main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (potato, carrot, peas, cauliflower, bell pepper)
Kolhapuri masala (coriander, cumin, red chilli, turmeric, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, poppy seeds)

serving.description: 1 plate of vegetable dish with accompanying bread or rice
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Veg Kolhapuri is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a generous portion of spiced mixed vegetable curry served alongside bread (roti/naan) or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India; and United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Veg Kolhapuri exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted composites derived from: (1) ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (2017) — mixed cooked vegetable curry (sabzi) entries for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron, and vitamin C; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — individual vegetable component entries (potato boiled, carrot cooked, green peas cooked, cauliflower cooked, tomato cooked) used to cross-validate macro and micronutrient estimates. The GrabFood input per_100g values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were used as a starting reference and reconciled against ICMR-NIN component data. Values are estimated composites and carry medium confidence. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary source for Indian dishes per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is an approved secondary global source per Section 5.1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Kolhapuri. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry with potato as the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, a GI of 55 falls in the LOW category (GI ≤ 55). Potato curry GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database range from 50–65 depending on preparation; a mid-range value of 55 is applied and classified as low per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 45.15) / 100 ≈ 24.8, rounded to 24.9. Serving carbohydrate = 12.9 g/100g × 350 g / 100 = 45.15 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 228.6 mg/100g; 800 mg per standard 350 g serving approaches 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 200 mg/100g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted bread; the dish's potassium content (~310 mg/100g from mixed vegetables) provides partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 55 (low boundary) driven by potato content; carbohydrate at 12.9 g/100g yields ~45 g per 350 g serving with a glycemic load of approximately 25
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request the dish with reduced potato and increased lower-GI vegetables (cauliflower, peas, bell pepper), and avoid pairing with large servings of white rice or refined bread to keep the overall meal glycemic load below 20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.3 g/100g is well below the 5 g threshold; trans fat is negligible at 0.06 g/100g; dietary fibre at 2.3 g/100g and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veg Kolhapuri is generally suitable for patients with cardiovascular disease when prepared with minimal oil; patients should request the dish cooked with less than 1 tablespoon of oil per serving and confirm no coconut cream is added, which would raise saturated fat significantly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade vegetables (tomato, potato, bell pepper, chilli) which may aggravate solanine-sensitive RA patients; turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients sensitive to solanine should request the dish without potato, eggplant, and bell pepper, and ask for moderate chilli level; turmeric and ginger in the Kolhapuri masala are evidence-based anti-inflammatory agents that may benefit RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Kolhapuri is a boldly spiced Indian vegetarian curry originating from Kolhapur, a city in Maharashtra, India, renowned for its fiery and aromatic cuisine. The dish consists of mixed vegetables — typically potato, carrot, green peas, cauliflower, and bell pepper — cooked in a rich, deeply flavoured gravy made from Kolhapuri masala, a complex spice blend incorporating dry-roasted red chillies, coriander, cumin, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, poppy seeds, and sesame seeds, combined with a tomato-onion base and finished with ginger and garlic. The cooking process involves dry-roasting and grinding the spice blend, sautéing the aromatics, and simmering the vegetables until tender in the spiced gravy. The result is a thick, intensely flavoured curry with a deep reddish-brown colour, a robust heat level, and a complex layered aroma. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 128.6 kcal, 3.4 g protein, 7.1 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 12.9 g carbohydrate, 2.3 g dietary fibre, and 228.6 mg sodium. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free. It carries a low glycemic index (estimated 55) at the low-medium boundary due to potato content, making portion control important for diabetic patients. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger are clinically relevant for inflammatory conditions. In Singapore, this dish appears on menus of Indian restaurants and occasionally on fusion menus. It is typically served with roti, naan, or steamed rice. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 350 g.
common_user_queries: Is Veg Kolhapuri suitable for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Veg Kolhapuri?
How many calories in Veg Kolhapuri?
Can hypertension patients eat Veg Kolhapuri?
What is the glycemic index of Veg Kolhapuri?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Veg Kolhapuri exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, USDA). Nutrient values are adapted composites from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data (approved primary source for Indian dishes) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy individual vegetable components (approved secondary source), cross-referenced against the GrabFood input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database potato curry data. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for mixed vegetable Kolhapuri curry is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite Veg Kolhapuri dish are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; individual component data could be summed in a future enrichment pass. 3. The input record described this as a Turkish/Kebab restaurant offering; however, the dish name and composition are unambiguously Maharashtrian Indian. Cuisine classification has been corrected to Indian (Maharashtrian) with region of origin Kolhapur, Maharashtra, India. 4. Cholesterol at 2.9 mg/100g is low and may reflect trace amounts from oil used in preparation; the dish is otherwise plant-based. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on potato-containing mixed vegetable curry from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables); no direct GI measurement for Veg Kolhapuri was found. GI 55 is classified as LOW per the standard scale (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70); gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration. 6. Potassium value of 310 mg/100g is an estimate derived from USDA SR Legacy data for the mixed vegetable components (potato, carrot, peas, cauliflower, tomato) weighted by approximate composition. 7. nutrient_source updated in this iteration to explicitly cite both ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved composite sources, replacing the previous citation that referenced only the ICMR-NIN dietary guidelines PDF URL (which was flagged as a forbidden/non-primary nutrient source). The authoritative ICMR-NIN nutrient tables publication and USDA FoodData Central are both approved sources per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1.

Updated gi_source notes to document the classification rationale. Updated notes field to document both fixes.

All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean-context Indian dish are present (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
